---
title: "Is grad school worth it?"
---

## Short answer: maybe.

Long answer: to model the opportunity cost of attending grad school, I use the
following analysis. Tweak the assumptions and watch the breakeven move.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof years = Inputs.range([1, 50], {value: 20, step: 1, label: "Years"})
```

### Scenario A: stay in industry
```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof currentIncome = Inputs.number({value: 75000, min: 0, step: 1000, label: "Current income ($)"})
viewof incomeGrowth = Inputs.number({value: 6.5, min: 0, step: 0.1, label: "Yearly income growth (%)"})
```

### Scenario B: go to grad school
```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof yearsOfSchool = Inputs.number({value: 2, min: 0, step: 1, label: "Years of school"})
viewof gradIncome = Inputs.number({value: -30000, step: 1000, label: "Grad school income ($)"})
viewof gradGrowth = Inputs.number({value: 0, min: 0, step: 0.1, label: "Grad school income growth (%)"})
viewof postGradIncome = Inputs.number({value: 100000, min: 0, step: 1000, label: "Post-grad income ($)"})
viewof postGradGrowth = Inputs.number({value: 8.5, min: 0, step: 0.1, label: "Post-grad income growth (%)"})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
// x points: `years` samples from 0..years (matches np.linspace(0, years, years))
xs = years <= 1 ? [0] : Array.from({length: years}, (_, i) => i * years / (years - 1))

A = x => currentIncome / 1000 * (1 + incomeGrowth / 100) ** x
B = x => x <= yearsOfSchool
  ? gradIncome / 1000 * (1 + gradGrowth / 100) ** x
  : postGradIncome / 1000 * (1 + postGradGrowth / 100) ** (x - (yearsOfSchool + 1))

cumsum = arr => arr.reduce((acc, v) => { acc.push((acc.length ? acc[acc.length - 1] : 0) + v); return acc; }, [])

a = xs.map(A)
b = xs.map(B)
cumA = cumsum(a).map(v => v / 100)   // in $100k
cumB = cumsum(b).map(v => v / 100)

// breakeven = first x where cumB crosses up through cumA (sign change of cumA-cumB)
breakeven = {
  const d = xs.map((_, i) => cumA[i] - cumB[i]);
  for (let i = 1; i < d.length; i++) {
    if (d[i - 1] > 0 && d[i] <= 0) {
      const t = d[i - 1] / (d[i - 1] - d[i]);     // linear interp to zero crossing
      return +(xs[i - 1] + t * (xs[i] - xs[i - 1])).toFixed(1);
    }
  }
  return null;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 820, height: 320, marginLeft: 55, grid: true,
  x: {label: "Years"}, y: {label: "Income (in $1k)"},
  marks: [
    Plot.lineY(xs.map((x, i) => ({x, y: a[i]})), {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "#1f77b4"}),
    Plot.lineY(xs.map((x, i) => ({x, y: b[i]})), {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "#d62728"}),
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.text([{x: xs[xs.length - 1], y: a[a.length - 1]}], {x: "x", y: "y", text: ["A: industry"], dx: -30, dy: -8, fill: "#1f77b4"}),
    Plot.text([{x: xs[xs.length - 1], y: b[b.length - 1]}], {x: "x", y: "y", text: ["B: grad school"], dx: -35, dy: 12, fill: "#d62728"})
  ]
})
```

The roots of A − B (opportunity cost) give us the breakeven point, if it exists:

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 820, height: 340, marginLeft: 55, grid: true,
  x: {label: "Years"}, y: {label: "Cumulative income (in $100k)"},
  marks: [
    Plot.lineY(xs.map((x, i) => ({x, y: cumA[i]})), {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "#1f77b4"}),
    Plot.lineY(xs.map((x, i) => ({x, y: cumB[i]})), {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "#d62728"}),
    breakeven ? Plot.ruleX([breakeven], {stroke: "black", strokeDasharray: "4 3"}) : null,
    breakeven ? Plot.text([{x: breakeven, y: 0}], {x: "x", y: "y", text: [`breakeven = ${breakeven} yrs`], dy: -10}) : null
  ].filter(d => d)
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
breakeven
  ? md`🎉 Congratulations! Going to grad school nets positive in **${breakeven} years**!`
  : md`No breakeven found... you'll never financially recover from this (try increasing the time horizon).`
```

Obviously, this toy model doesn't capture the risks involved in each scenario,
which can be great depending on your situation. But what it does provide is some
insight into the sensitivity of your outcomes to these limited assumptions. In my
case (not pictured), the answer to whether grad school was worth it (financially)
is a resounding NO!

But will that stop me? Stay tuned...
